summary: Gruber shares an anecdote about the surprisingly loud fan noise in Apple's 2005 Developer Transition Kit machines — Intel PCs housed in Power Mac cases given to developers during the PowerPC-to-Intel transition. The story comes via Cabel Sasser of Panic, prompted by Craig Hockenberry's concern about noise levels in his newly quieted office. Sasser's team filmed video evidence of the DTK's fan noise to address Hockenberry's fears, which apparently confirmed his worst expectations. The piece is framed as a humorous contrast to Apple's usual hardware refinement, with a topical connection to Psystar's notoriously noisy Mac clones.
key_points: Apple's Developer Transition Kits were essentially PC hardware in Power Mac cases, not refined Apple products
Craig Hockenberry had undertaken a project to minimize office noise and feared the DTK would ruin it
The DTK's fan noise was so bad that Hockenberry's reaction contained 'unprintable' all-caps comments
The story parallels Psystar's clone machines, which were also criticized for egregious fan noise
money_quotes: I encouraged Sasser to publish this important story on his own well-regarded Cabel's Blog LOL, but, alas, due to scheduling constraints at Panic, he estimated such publication wouldn't be feasible for another few years.
He fully expected it to be the normal PC POS hardware.
We took a quick video to quell his fears.
Suffice it to say Craig's response contained all-caps comments that are unprintable here.
We waited a little while, then sent him a follow-up video with a little more… explanation.
key_insight: Even Apple's own interim PC hardware during the Intel transition was the kind of loud, unrefined experience that underscores why Apple's obsessive hardware engineering matters.
